diary:
Grey drizzle followed me all day, like the city was keeping a steady drumbeat. I woke up in Redmond to that soft rain sound, made coffee, did a quick tidy, and checked the ferry schedule like it was a ritual. By the time I hit the road toward Sea-Tac, the SR-520 eastbound closure had already started to ripple through traffic—nothing catastrophic, just that annoying extra crawl and headlights everywhere. At work I settled in, stared at the boards, and handled arrivals/departures through the steady wet weather. The runway felt busy in a quieter way, if that makes sense.

Around Denny’s I watched quick weather/aviation updates and tried not to let the gloom get into my head. Later, I grabbed indoor gardening supplies at REI, then mapped out a soil plan at home. Climbing at Sender One was a relief—technique over ego. Friends at Downtown Redmond Park stayed mostly indoors, and we even talked ferries like it mattered. By the time I cooked and did one last check on the couch, I felt oddly calm, like I’d threaded the day together on purpose.
thought_process: I chose to lean into small routines today because the weather and the bridge closure were basically asking for patience. The SR-520 situation made my commute riskier, so I left early, kept routing cautious, and tried to treat delays as “expected friction,” not something to fight. At Sea-Tac, steady rain means predictable complications—visibility and spacing—so I focused on flow and breaks instead of overthinking individual moments. Lunch and the quick cafe updates were about staying current without draining my attention. The gardening errand and the climbing were mood resets: both are tactile, controllable, and they balance the sterile intensity of ATC. World events didn’t intrude directly, but the infrastructure hiccup definitely nudged my stress up, then back down after I got home and locked in the evening plan.
world_event_prompt: Grey, drizzly Tuesday with steady rain from 7am to 2pm, high of 58F. SR-520 bridge closed eastbound 9am-noon for emergency repair, snarling cross-lake traffic.
